sound_type: three-note hoo-HOO-hoo -- distinctive rhythmic cooing with emphasis on second note at 400-700 Hz, repeated persistently from rooftops and power lines; E=3
ecosystem: Urban and suburban habitats. Strongly associated with human settlement -- grain elevators, feedlots, bird feeders, residential areas. Avoids deep forest. Invasive species in North America. One of the fastest avian range expansions in history.
season: Year-round resident and caller. Breeding year-round in mild climates. The most persistent Columbidae caller -- calling bouts of 10-30 minutes.
family: Columbidae (3rd entry. THIRD COLUMBIDAE. First non-native. Invasive.)

coordinates.description: Eurasian Collared-Doves are found in urban and suburban areas throughout the PNW, particularly near grain sources, feedlots, and bird feeders.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRD Columbidae entry. FIRST non-native Columbidae. Invasive species. Reached North America via Bahamas pet shop escape (~1975), Florida (~1982), PNW (~2000). One of the fastest avian range expansions in modern history. 100-150 km/year expansion rate.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Columbidae: third entry. Band-tailed (149) + Mourning Dove (150) + Eurasian Collared-Dove (151).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Streptopelia from Greek 'streptos' (collar, twisted necklace) + 'peleia' (dove) -- the collared dove. decaocto from Greek 'dekaokto' (eighteen) -- from a myth about a servant paid 18 coins whose cries were transformed into the dove's call.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Streptopelia -- first entry. The 'collared doves' -- Old World genus. Asian/European origin.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Third Columbidae. First non-native. Native/non-native boundary crossed. Invasive species.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Coo: 400-700 Hz. Second note may reach 800 Hz. Slightly higher than Mourning Dove.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Cooing: 400-700 Hz. Harsh flight call: 1000-2000 Hz.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Three-note phrase: 1.5-2.0 seconds. Repeated with 2-5 second pauses. Calling bouts: 10-30 minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: THREE-NOTE COO: 'hoo-HOO-hoo' -- emphasis on second note (higher and louder). Invariable. Diagnostic. No native NA dove has this pattern.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Three-note phrase repeated dozens of times with short pauses. Most persistent Columbidae caller. Monotonous repetition.
song_metrics.timbre: Moderate, rhythmic, slightly harsh compared to Mourning Dove. More assertive. Less breathy, more emphatic.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Moderate. Audible 100-250m in urban conditions. Louder than Mourning Dove. Still ambient background. Near the upper E=3 boundary.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate repetition rate. 2-5 second pauses. Extended calling bouts of 10-30 minutes unusual for Columbidae.
song_metrics.context: Year-round calling. Rooftops, power lines, TV antennas, tall trees near buildings. Urban/suburban matrix. Most persistent caller of the family.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Three-note pattern consistent across expanding range.
song_metrics.key_property: THREE-NOTE HOO-HOO-HOO -- 400-700 Hz, persistent, monotonous, the sound of invasion. E=3. THIRD COLUMBIDAE. FIRST NON-NATIVE. Fastest avian range expansion. Florida to PNW in 20 years. This sound did not exist in the PNW before ~2000.

plumage.overall: Pale buff-gray, uniform coloring. Stocky build. Square, white-tipped tail. Black crescent on nape (diagnostic field mark -- 'the collar'). Larger than Mourning Dove.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Black crescent collar on back of neck. Square white-tipped tail. Stocky build. No native NA dove has the collar.
plumage.flight_pattern: Direct, somewhat labored. Harsh nasal flight call often given on takeoff.

physical_description.overall: Length: 29-33 cm. Weight: 150-240 g. Larger than Mourning Dove (100-170g). Stocky, chunky build vs. Mourning Dove's slim elegance.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Black neck collar (crescent on nape). Square white-tipped tail. Pale buff-gray. Stocky build. Urban habitat.
physical_description.field_id: Stocky pale dove on rooftops or power lines near buildings. Look for black collar on nape. Three-note rhythmic call. Often near grain sources. Larger and paler than Mourning Dove.

nodes.title: Fastest Avian Range Expansion
nodes.description: Bahamas pet shop escape (~1975) to continental coverage in <30 years. Florida ~1982, Gulf Coast 1990, PNW ~2000. 100-150 km/year. Fueled by human commensalism, grain economy, year-round breeding, no predator pressure at front.

nodes.title: A New Sound in the Soundscape
nodes.description: The three-note 'hoo-HOO-hoo' did not exist in the PNW before ~2000. The engine documents the acoustic landscape as it is, including invasive sounds. The Collared-Dove's call is an acoustic fact of the modern PNW.

nodes.title: Native/Non-Native Boundary Crossed
nodes.description: First non-native Columbidae. The transition from native (149-150) to non-native (151-152). The progression tells a displacement and colonization story. The engine documents without editorial judgment.

nodes.title: Three Columbidae at E=3
nodes.description: Band-tailed (E=3, deep/muted), Mourning Dove (E=3, soft/mournful), Collared-Dove (E=3, persistent/monotonous). Three mechanisms, same energy. Family energy signature. Only Rock Pigeon wing clap (E=4) will break it.

cross_references.relationship: NATIVE vs. INVASIVE! Both Columbidae, both E=3. Mourning Dove (native, 300M, centuries of presence) vs. Collared-Dove (invasive, 30 years, explosive growth). Potential competition in suburban habitat.
NATIVE ISOLATION! Band-tailed in forest, Collared-Dove in urban/agricultural. Minimal direct competition. Different habitats protect the native.
NON-NATIVE PAIR! Both non-native Columbidae in urban areas. Collared-Dove (invasive, vocal, 30 years) and Rock Pigeon (feral, mechanical, centuries). Different origins, same cities.
